problem_summary: 2022 MCM/ICM Problem F (policy type): asteroid mining and global equity. The team had to (1) define and build a quantitative model of "global equity," (2) describe the future of the asteroid-mining industry and assess its effect on equity, (3) explain the mechanisms by which mining changes equity and how shifting scenarios alter it, and (4) recommend updated outer-space-treaty policy to the UN. The paper's central move is to treat each country as an "individual" in an international income distribution, measure each country's "income" as a composite national-power score, and feed that into an entropy-based inequality index to make the abstract notion of fairness measurable and trackable over time.
models_used: Modified/decomposable Theil index (entropy-based inequality measure) as the core equity metric — extended from individual incomes to nations via a population-weighted variant T' = (n_i/N) sum (y_i/ybar) ln(y_i/ybar) and a log-normalized correction T*' = T'/(lnN - T') to remove population-size dependence

Factor analysis (SAS-computed) on 12 national-power variables (GDP, territory, military, technology, population, health, education, official expenses, per-capita production, telephone lines, international trade, internet population) reduced to m=3 common factors capturing >80% variance, producing a normalized composite national-power score F in [0,1)

n-oligopoly Cournot-style competitive game with inverse demand P(x)=gamma - sum(x_i), comparing Nash equilibrium (x=(gamma-c)/(n+1)) vs full-cooperation outcome (x=(gamma-c)/2n) to argue cooperation dominates competition

Cooperative game / Shapley value model with characteristic-function superadditivity constraints (v(S1 ∪ S2) >= v(S1)+v(S2)), reformulated as a multi-objective program minimizing the Theil index subject to GDP-update and factor-score constraints
Multi-objective programming coupling the Shapley allocation to the equity objective (target = min of the normalized Theil index)
Monte Carlo simulation to sweep mining vs non-mining country contribution levels and produce a revenue heat map (optimal Shapley contributions ~0.6437 / 0.5982)

Path analysis (structural simple-correlation path equations) to decompose how a mining-profit variable W propagates through the 12 indicators to the equity score; top path coefficients 0.45 (GDP), 0.38 (per-capita income/production), 0.29 (international trade)
Gini coefficient computed in MATLAB as an independent cross-check on the Theil-based equity story
Theil decomposition into within-group (TWR) and between-group (TBR) components (Theil = TWR + TBR), implemented in Stata
key_techniques: The defining intellectual move: a country-as-individual mapping (population -> number of countries, income -> composite national power) that converts an undefined 'fairness' concept into a standard income-inequality problem with off-the-shelf metrics

Choosing the Theil index specifically for its decomposability (within- vs between-group), then exploiting that decomposition to separate equity among mining countries, among non-mining countries, and between the two groups

Inventing concrete fictional but quantified shocks ('Lucky', 'Lucky2', 'Lucky3' minerals with specified effects, e.g. +15% GDP or +0.05 to normalized indicators) to make an unknowable future tractable, then validating against REAL historical 1975-2016 data as the comparison baseline

Grounding every speculative scenario in a real historical analogue: European Coal and Steel Community for the cooperation governance structure, OPEC for monopolistic interest groups, the Second Industrial Revolution (France 1870-1897 manufacturing growth) as the growth rate for a 'rising nation' scenario, and the documented 'resource curse' literature
Building a single reusable evaluation pipeline (factor score -> Theil index) and reapplying it across all four problems so results compose coherently rather than being four disconnected analyses
Anchoring the policy answer (Problem 4) directly to the structure of the actual UN Charter preamble, quoting its clauses and deriving each policy recommendation from a specific charter principle
Multi-method triangulation: confirming the Theil findings against an independently-computed Gini coefficient
why_it_won: From a judging perspective this paper wins on the hardest part of a vague policy problem: operationalizing an abstract, contested concept ('global equity') into something measurable, testable, and traceable. The country-as-individual + factor-score + Theil-index construction is genuinely clever and self-consistent, and the team made it the spine of all four answers so the paper reads as one connected argument rather than four mini-papers. Crucially, the team handled the 'unknowable future' problem the way judges reward: they invented explicit, quantified hypothetical shocks but always benchmarked them against REAL historical data (1975-2016 Theil/Gini trends, ECSC, OPEC, the Second Industrial Revolution, the resource curse), so speculation stays disciplined by reality. The modeling is methodologically diverse and appropriate (factor analysis for dimension reduction, game theory for the competition->cooperation narrative, Shapley for fair allocation, path analysis for mechanism, Monte Carlo for robustness) yet each method feeds the same equity metric. They also did real validation work — historical test, regional test, sensitivity test (±5% indicator perturbation), and a Gini cross-check — which signals scientific rigor. Finally, Problem 4's tight mapping of each recommendation to a quoted UN Charter clause gives the policy section authority and structure that most teams lack.
section_highlights: The Summary is strong: it walks problem-by-problem and names the exact methods and headline numbers (path coefficients 0.45/0.38/0.29) so a judge sees the whole arc in one page. The 'Our work' full-text flowchart (Figure 2) gives an early roadmap of how every model connects. Section 6 (equity model) is the standout: it motivates the metric from a dictionary definition of equity, exposes the naive Theil index's flaw (it would call the US-vs-Vatican comparison 'most fair'), and fixes it with a population weighting and log-normalization — showing critical self-awareness judges love. The model-test trio (historical / regional / sensitivity) plus the Gini cross-check is a textbook validation block. Section 7's narrative 'from competition to cooperation -> but monopoly spikes inequity -> so design a cooperative Shapley system' is a clean, persuasive storyline. The governance structure borrowed from the ECSC (Figure 11) gives a concrete institutional design rather than hand-waving. Section 9 structures policy as a clause-by-clause response to the UN Charter preamble. The explicit Strengths/Weaknesses section is honest (it admits repeated Monte Carlo use weakens generality and that the model has a 'time limit'). Stata code is included in the appendix for reproducibility.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Very small sample for the national-power factor analysis — only 8 countries for 12 variables, which is statistically far too few stable factor loadings; factor scores and the >80%-variance claim are not credibly supported at that n

Many figures show implausibly flat / clearly fabricated-looking time series: the world Gini is pinned at exactly 0.435 for ~35 consecutive years, and several Theil curves are frozen constant out to 2090, suggesting placeholder or hand-drawn data rather than computed output (one chart even carries a note that values are 'estimated based on the provided code snippet')

The Monte Carlo 'revenue heat map' (Figure 12) is a simple additive grid (cell = row+col scaled), not an actual stochastic simulation — the headline optimal contributions 0.6437/0.5982 are asserted without showing the simulation machinery

Heavy reliance on invented minerals (Lucky/Lucky2/Lucky3) with arbitrarily chosen effect sizes (+15% GDP, +0.05 to indicators) means key conclusions hinge on unjustified magnitude assumptions; the team itself flags that repeated Monte Carlo use weakens generality
The oligopoly game uses an identical-cost, symmetric Cournot setup that doesn't reflect the huge asymmetries between spacefaring nations the paper otherwise stresses
Path-analysis result is somewhat circular: W is defined to raise the indicators, so finding that GDP/trade/income are the top channels is partly built into the construction
Notable writing/translation roughness ('sharply model' for Shapley, 'through-hole/flux diameter' for path analysis, typos like 'STATULK') — tolerated by judges but a polish risk
Equity is reduced almost entirely to economic national power; non-economic dimensions of fairness (rights, environment, intergenerational) appear only qualitatively in policy
reusable_patterns: The 'analogy mapping' template for any define-the-concept policy problem: map the fuzzy target onto a well-studied quantitative domain (here income inequality) so you inherit its established metrics, decompositions, and tests

Pick a metric for a property you'll exploit later — they chose Theil over Gini specifically for decomposability, then used within/between decomposition to answer Problem 2; let the downstream analysis dictate the metric choice

Dimension-reduce many candidate indicators with factor analysis (or PCA) into one composite score, then make that score the common currency every later model reads and writes — gives a coherent, composable pipeline across all sub-problems

Handle an unknowable future by inventing explicit, named, quantified hypothetical shocks, but always validate the model against real historical data as the baseline — speculation disciplined by reality
For every speculative scenario, attach a concrete historical analogue (ECSC, OPEC, an industrial revolution, the resource curse) to make claims credible and grounded
Triangulate the headline metric with a second independent measure (Theil + Gini) to demonstrate robustness
Standard validation battery for an evaluation metric: historical test + regional/grouping test + ±X% sensitivity perturbation + cross-method check

For a UN/policy deliverable, structure recommendations as a clause-by-clause response to the actual governing document (UN Charter preamble) so the policy section has built-in authority and organization
Open with a problem-by-problem summary that names methods AND headline numbers, and include a full-text flowchart so judges grasp the model architecture before reading details
Include runnable code (Stata/MATLAB) in the appendix to signal reproducibility
